title: Electron-photon interaction: Feynman diagrams and contact points
abstract: In this note, we formulate the notions of the direct and inverse problems and contact points for the Feynman diagrams. For the electron-photon interaction case, the solutions of these direct and inverse problems are presented. The interrelations between Feynman diagrams and the classical colored graphs are discussed.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical framework for relating the internal, unobservable components of Feynman diagrams to their external, observable counterparts. It establishes a formal link between the topological properties of these diagrams and the convergence properties of the associated Feynman integrals.
main_problem: The paper addresses the formulation and solution of the direct and inverse problems for Feynman diagrams, specifically focusing on recovering internal virtual particle data from external observable data and vice versa.
main_result: The author proves that complete direct data can be recovered from inverse data and vice versa, and identifies exactly four specific regimes of divergence for the Feynman integrals.
method: The study employs graph theory (Betti numbers, cyclomatic numbers) and topological characterization to map the relationship between Feynman diagrams and classical colored graphs.
model_or_system: The system is the electron-photon interaction within the framework of Quantum Electrodynamics (QED), modeled as an m-fold integral of a rational matrix function over a 4m-fold sphere.
key_observables: External electron lines (Ne) and external photon lines (Np).
important_parameters: Number of vertices (n), number of internal electron/photon lines (Fe, Fp), number of loops (m), and the counter number (K) which determines convergence.
main_assumptions: The Feynman diagrams are assumed to be irreducible, and the model assumes a specific vertex structure where two electron lines and one photon line meet.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the direct and inverse problems, introduces the mathematical model for electron-photon interaction, applies graph-theoretic methods to characterize the diagrams, and concludes with the classification of divergence regimes and data recovery proofs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
